name: Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone (TSH)
summary: TSH is a pituitary hormone that signals the thyroid gland to make thyroid hormones (T4 and T3). It is the primary screening test for thyroid function.
whatItMeans: TSH works on a feedback loop: when thyroid hormone is low, the pituitary releases more TSH to push the thyroid, and when thyroid hormone is high, TSH falls. Because of this inverse relationship, a high TSH often points toward an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) and a low TSH toward an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism). A commonly cited adult reference range is about 0.4–4.0 µIU/mL, though laboratories and age groups vary. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Primary hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid, e.g. Hashimoto's thyroiditis)
Older age (reference range tends to shift slightly upward)
lowers: Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid, e.g. Graves' disease, toxic nodule)
confounders: Biotin supplements can interfere with immunoassays, producing falsely low (or sometimes altered) TSH results — pause biotin before testing
TSH varies with time of day (typically higher overnight/early morning, lower in the afternoon)
Acute or severe non-thyroidal illness can transiently suppress or distort TSH (sick euthyroid)
Reference intervals rise with age, so a fixed cutoff may misclassify older adults

ranges.note: Common adult reference range ~0.4–4.0 µIU/mL (equivalent to mIU/L). Laboratory- and age-specific ranges vary; pregnancy uses trimester-specific intervals.

references.title: Aberrant Thyroid Function Tests in a Patient Taking Biotin Supplements (PMC)
